Alright, so with that now, let's move forward into the meat and potatoes of the rest of this module. And it's about two things. Number one, mastering the art of learning how to read from a script without sounding like you're reading from a script. That's number one. And then number two, learning how to actually construct a script so you can do number one. You see, if number two is not in place, if you write a script using the wrong methodology, using things that don't allow you to sound fluid, then there's simply no way that you can sound like you're supposed to sound like an expert, natural, elegant, right? So those two things go together. So the rest of this module now is about the construction and the delivery of straight line scripts. I'm going to break it down for you into eight different rules here. And we'll go one by one. I'll dig into each one. And then we'll take some quizzes along the way. Some of them are pretty in-depth here. But let's start with number one. Now the first rule here is that a straight line script is not front-loaded. In other words, your scripts are really a series of scripts. You don't go all the way with one script and just keep talk, talk, talk, talk, talk, and then S for the order. We call this framing. We frame but don't front-load. Imagine constructing a house. You're building a house, right? So what do you do? You pour the foundation, then you put up the frame. That's step one, right? Then after the frame is up, then you start putting in the plumbing, the electric, you put the drywall in, right? Well imagine this. When you are creating your straight line scripts, your initial presentation, the main body, essentially is when you're framing your airtight logical case. You're explaining why your product is awesome, but you're not giving the prospect everything in one shot. If you do, what will happen is it's too much information. The prospect becomes overwhelmed and also you can't get someone from, let's say, a five on the certainty scale to an absolute 10 in one shot. That's just not how human beings are built. We need to get certain in stages. It's like imagine like a race car, you know, when it has gears, right? So it goes, and then it stops, it goes, so it kind of backs off for a second, takes a chance to catch his breath, the car, and then lurches forward again. And then it goes, so that sound that they backing off sound, that's the point in time in the scripting analogy here where your prospects, the chance to digest what you said. And then after that, you then move to an even higher level of certain. So they might go from a five to an eight with your first presentation. And then they might give you an objection. And then they'll fall back now to a seven or 6.5. But the next time you fill in with your next pattern, it'll take them to a nine or 9.5. In the sense that with each successive script, it's a series of scripts, you keep moving your prospects to a higher and higher level of certainty. So that's why we don't front load off scripts. Front loading means you have to throw the kitchen sink of everything at them. And it's like a 12, 20 minute proposition. They're like tuned out and tired by the time you're done. We give them a framed logical case and then we fill in the blanks. That's number one. Now, moving on to number two, you want to not be focusing on the features. You want to focus on benefits. Now, obviously this is sales 101, but for some inexplicable reason, when I see untrained sales people making pictures, they go on and on about feature after feature after feature. And it literally just blows up the sale quotes. The prospect will tune out. So what you want to do here is you can't just talk about benefits because that would be ridiculous. There's no context. It's interesting. It does this that you're going to have actual features that you mentioned and then you want to expand on that feature by citing all the benefits. So it doesn't buy a TV set because the picture moves at 86 gigahertz or whatever it might be, right? They buy because it looks awesome, makes them feel great. They get emotionally connected. The benefit to the person is a great watching experience, great viewing experience. So the feature needs to be mentioned, but you want to expand on the benefits. And those are your first two rules here. Okay? Now we're going to break this up here, take a short quiz, make sure you lock this stuff in and let's come right back and keep going. I'll see you in a few minutes.
